input.old_string:
# Optional shared-system overlay — best-effort. Backs /workspace/.system with the
# single /_system subtree (INDEX.md, GUIDE.md docs, builtin skills). If /_system
# doesn't exist or the mount fails, per-user workspaces keep their own copies.
mkdir -p "$WORKSPACE/.system"
chown "$SANDBOX_UID:$SANDBOX_GID" "$WORKSPACE/.system" 2>/dev/null || true
if wait_mounted "$JFS_SYSTEM_MOUNT" 30 && mount --bind "$JFS_SYSTEM_MOUNT" "$WORKSPACE/.system"; then
    mount -o remount,bind,ro "$WORKSPACE/.system" 2>/dev/null || true
fi
input.new_string:
# Shared-system overlay — best-effort. Backs /workspace/.system with the system
# files (INDEX.md, GUIDE.md docs, builtin skills) that the per-user symlinks
# target. These are static + identical for every user, so we prefer the copy
# BAKED into the E2B template at /etc/gaia/system — a plain read-only bind, no
# JuiceFS client and no per-sandbox metadata-engine session. Older templates
# (pre-bake) have no /etc/gaia/system, so fall back to the JuiceFS /_system mount.
mkdir -p "$WORKSPACE/.system"
chown "$SANDBOX_UID:$SANDBOX_GID" "$WORKSPACE/.system" 2>/dev/null || true
if [[ -d /etc/gaia/system ]]; then
    if mount --bind /etc/gaia/system "$WORKSPACE/.system"; then
        mount -o remount,bind,ro "$WORKSPACE/.system" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
else
    launch_system_subdir
    if wait_mounted "$JFS_SYSTEM_MOUNT" 30 && mount --bind "$JFS_SYSTEM_MOUNT" "$WORKSPACE/.system"; then
        mount -o remount,bind,ro "$WORKSPACE/.system" 2>/dev/null || true
    fi
fi

# Each launch_*_subdir only STARTS the juicefs daemon (launch_juicefs is
# setsid+&, non-blocking) — it does NOT wait for readiness. The three are all
# independent clients of the same volume on different mountpoints, and each pays
# ~13s of connect + SQL schema-sync. Launching them together lets that overlap,
# so the whole mount waits ~once (~14s) instead of summing (~47s). The
# wait_mounted + bind for each happens in the main flow below.
#
# Memory: the E2B sandbox has only ~1 GB RAM and runs all THREE daemons at once.
# --buffer-size is an in-RAM read/write buffer, so the old 600+300+300 MB OOM-
# killed the mount ~2/3 of the time. Keep the buffers small (128+32+32) so they
# coexist in ~1 GB; reads go host-side in prod so a big sandbox buffer buys little.
launch_user_subdir() {
    mountpoint -q "$JFS_MOUNT" && return 0

# setsid+&, non-blocking) — it does NOT wait for readiness. The three are all
# independent clients of the same volume on different mountpoints, and each pays
# ~13s of connect + SQL schema-sync. Launching them together lets that overlap,
# so the whole mount waits ~once (~14s) instead of summing (~47s). The
# wait_mounted + bind for each happens in the main flow below.
#
# Memory: the E2B sandbox has only ~1 GB RAM and runs all THREE daemons at once.
# --buffer-size is an in-RAM read/write buffer, so the old 600+300+300 MB OOM-
# killed the mount ~2/3 of the time. Keep the buffers small (128+32+32) so they
# coexist in ~1 GB; reads go host-side in prod so a big sandbox buffer buys little.

content.text:
All three changes are implemented and verified (type-check ✓, lint ✓, bash syntax ✓, bake smoke-test ✓ — 63 system files tar'd with the correct `/workspace/.system/<rel>` paths).

## Answering your two questions

**Why can't #1 and #2 merge?** They're **sibling top-level prefixes** in the JuiceFS namespace — `/users/<uid>` (RW) and `/skills/<uid>` (RO), not parent/child. The primary mount is `--subdir /users/<uid>`, which by design *cannot see* `/skills/<uid>`. Even if you relocated skills under `/users/<uid>/skills`, you'd lose the **hard read-only guarantee**: the skills mount is `--read-only` at the FUSE layer so the in-sandbox agent can't tamper with API-managed (Mongo-is-truth) skill bodies, and the bind-remount-ro fallback is unreliable on older kernels (per the script's own comment). So merging trades a real integrity property for one daemon — not worth it. `/_system` is different: it's static + shared, so it can leave JuiceFS entirely.

**Does baking slow init?** No — faster and more reliable. It removes a `wait_mounted` poll and a daemon competing for the sandbox's ~1 GB RAM (the script notes 3 concurrent daemons OOM-killed mounts ~2/3 of the time). E2B boots from a prebuilt snapshot, so a few KB of baked files cost nothing at boot.

## What I changed

1. **`--no-bgjob` on the RW sandbox mount** (`mount_juicefs.sh`). The verified root cause: `--read-only` *already* implies `--no-bgjob`, so the two RO mounts were never the problem — the per-sandbox **primary RW mount** was each independently running the stale-session GC scan (`SMEMBERS`/`ZSCORE` over the whole session set). Now the host-side whole-volume mount is the single GC owner; sandboxes opt out. **This is the fix for your 800k.**
2. **Graceful unmount on eviction** (`lifecycle.py` `_hard_evict`): unmount the JuiceFS daemons before `sandbox.kill()` so sessions deregister instead of leaking and being re-scanned.
3. **Bake `/_system` into the template** (`build_e2b_template.py` + `mount_juicefs.sh`): static system files ship at `/etc/gaia/system`, bind-mounted RO into `/workspace/.system` — no daemon. I used a **backward-compatible rollout** (prefer baked dir, fall back to the JuiceFS `/_system` mount on older templates) because `mount.sh` is runtime-shipped while the baked dir needs a template rebuild — this avoids a deploy-ordering break. Storage-side code is untouched.

## Action required from you

- **Rebuild the E2B template** for #3 to take effect: `cd apps/api && uv run python scripts/build_e2b_template.py`, then set the new `E2B_TEMPLATE_ID`. Until then, sandboxes safely use the JuiceFS fallback.
- Once every environment is on a rebuilt template, a **follow-up** can delete the fallback (`launch_system_subdir`, `JFS_SYSTEM_MOUNT`) and the now-unused host-side `ensure_system_subtree`/`init_system_subtree` machinery + its test.
- I could **not** runtime-verify #2/#3 against a live sandbox here (needs E2B). The mechanism (#3 tarball) is smoke-tested; the in-sandbox bind + unmount behavior should be confirmed on the rebuilt template.

Nothing committed/pushed. Want me to commit these, or hold while you rebuild the template and test?

input.prompt:
I'm optimizing how the GAIA API syncs a user's workspace files (skills, system docs, integration catalogs) onto JuiceFS, to avoid redundant work that generates JuiceFS metadata traffic. I need a precise map of WHEN sync runs and WHAT gating already exists.

Investigate apps/api (FastAPI). Focus:

1. Find `provision_user_workspace` and `sync_user_workspaces` / `sync_stale_user_workspaces` (likely in app/services/workspace_sync.py and app/services/storage/sessions/). Read them.

2. Determine the TRIGGER POINTS — where is workspace/skill provisioning invoked from? Specifically:
   - On every sandbox acquire? (check app/services/sandbox/lifecycle.py acquire_sandbox path and app/services/storage/sessions/lifecycle.py)
   - On every chat turn / agent run?
   - At startup only?
   - On integration connect/disconnect events?
   Trace the actual callers with file:line.

3. What gating/short-circuiting already exists to skip work when nothing changed? Look for:
   - hash markers: SKILLS_HASH_MARKER, read_skills_marker/write_skills_marker, _library_signature, the ".gaia/skills.v" marker
   - matches_text hash comparisons in materialize_skills (app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py)
   - any "signature" or "version" gating
   Explain exactly what each gate compares and what I/O it still does even on the no-op path (e.g. does it still stat/read marker files on JuiceFS on every call?).

4. The key question: on a STEADY-STATE sandbox acquire (skills unchanged), how many JuiceFS filesystem operations (stat/read/scandir/write) does the provision path still perform? Is the gate cheap (one marker read) or does it walk the whole skill tree every time?

Report concrete file:line references, the gating logic, and the residual per-acquire I/O cost on the unchanged path. Do NOT propose fixes — just give me the factual map. Be thorough; read the real files.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have enough information. Let me compile the comprehensive report:

## GAIA API Workspace Sync Investigation Report

### 1. TRIGGER POINTS — Where Workspace Syncing Is Invoked

#### **Startup (Main App Only)**
- **File:** `app/core/provider_registration.py:257`
- **Trigger:** `resync_stale_user_workspaces()` spawned as background task during `unified_startup(context="main_app")`
- **Path:** `provider_registration.py:257` → spawns `resync_stale_user_workspaces()` → calls `sync_stale_user_workspaces(active_only=True)` (workspace_sync.py:119)

#### **User Registration/Signup**
- **File:** `app/services/oauth/oauth_service.py:136`
- **Trigger:** After successful user creation in `store_user_info()`, fire-and-forget call to `schedule_user_provision(str(result.inserted_id))`
- **Details:** Non-blocking; runs in background. Calls `_provision(user_id)` which calls `provision_user_workspace(user_id)` with no integration IDs (workspace_sync.py:37-40)

#### **Integration Connect/Disconnect**
- **File:** `app/services/integrations/user_integration_status.py:65`
- **Trigger:** When `update_user_integration_status()` sets status to `"connected"`, immediately calls `schedule_user_integrations_sync(user_id)`
- **Path:** `user_integration_status.py:65` → `schedule_user_integrations_sync()` (from integrations_fs.py:27-29) → `sync_user_integrations(user_id)` → `materialize_user_integrations(user_id, connected_ids)`
- **Details:** Fire-and-forget scheduler. Also called on integration disconnect (when status changes from connected).

#### **Sandbox Acquire — Pre-Creation on Host Only (NOT on Cache Hit)**
- **File:** `app/services/sandbox/lifecycle.py:530`
- **Trigger:** When creating a FRESH sandbox (not reusing cached), calls `_seed_user_subtrees(user_id)` which calls:
  - `ensure_user_workspace(user_id)` — creates `{MOUNT}/users/{user_id}` + `.gaia` dir
  - `ensure_user_skills_dir(user_id)` — creates `{MOUNT}/skills/{user_id}` dir
- **Context:** Runs on HOST side (juicefs.py:111-134), not in sandbox. Only for fresh create; cached sandboxes skip this entirely.
- **I/O:** Just `mkdir` operations, no sync of skill contents.

#### **Startup Stale-Scan (Active Users Only)**
- **File:** `app/services/workspace_sync.py:61-103`
- **Logic:** 
  1. On startup, scan users with conversation activity in past N days (settings.SESSION_RETENTION_DAYS, default)
  2. For each user, read `.gaia/skills.v` marker and compare to current `library_hash()`
  3. If mismatch OR `force=True`: call `provision_user_workspace(user_id, connected_ids)`
  4. Handles one deploy-time skill update → reshuffles all active users in background

### 2. GATING/SHORT-CIRCUITING LOGIC — What Avoids Redundant Work

#### **Gate 1: Skills Library Hash (Global)**
- **Marker:** `.gaia/skills.v` under user workspace root
- **Read Location:** `app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py:46-54` (`read_skills_marker()`)
- **Compare:** `workspace_sync.py:85` and `lifecycle.py:63` read marker, compare to `library_hash()` (from `app/agents/workspace/skill_loader.py`)
- **Behavior:**
  - If marker matches current hash → entire `_materialize_if_stale()` returns early at lifecycle.py:74 (no I/O on the "no change" path)
  - If marker missing or mismatch → proceeds to materialize

**I/O Cost (No-Op Path):**
- 1x `read()` call on `.gaia/skills.v` (one stat + read, typically <100 bytes)
- 1x `read()` call on `.gaia/connected.v` marker (connected integration set; typically <1KB)
- 1x `read()` call on `.gaia/instructions.v` marker (instructions hash; typically <64 bytes)
- **Total: 3 small marker reads on steady-state**

#### **Gate 2: Connected Integration Set Signature**
- **Marker:** `.gaia/connected.v` under user workspace root
- **Compare:** `lifecycle.py:66-68` builds `",".join(sorted(connected))` signature, compares at line 71
- **Behavior:** If this differs, proceeds to rewrite SKILL.md catalog (even if library hash matched)

#### **Gate 3: Per-User Instructions Hash**
- **Marker:** `.gaia/instructions.v` under user workspace root
- **Compare:** `lifecycle.py:68` and `72` — compares instructions signature
- **Behavior:** If this differs, proceeds to rewrite instructions.md files

**Combined Gate Logic (lifecycle.py:69-74):**
```python
if (
    current == expected_hash  # Gate 1: library hash
    and previous_connected == connected_signature  # Gate 2: integration set
    and previous_instructions == instructions_sig  # Gate 3: instructions
):
    return  # EARLY EXIT — no I/O below
```
All three must match to early-exit. If ANY differ, materializers run.

#### **Gate 4: Per-File Hash (Inside Materializers)**
- **Location:** `app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py:72-74` (`_write_skill_dir()`) and line 117, 170
- **Function:** `app/services/storage/_vfs_common.py:169-182` (`matches_text()`)
- **Behavior:**
  - Before writing each skill body file (SKILL.md), read target file and compare hash
  - If content unchanged → skip write, count stays at 0
  - **BUT:** This comparison STILL HAPPENS even when Gate 1-3 matched (no early exit above)
  
**Critical Issue:** If Gate 1-3 early-exit fires, `materialize_skills()` and `materialize_instructions()` are never called, so per-file reads are skipped.
However, if Gate 1-3 mismatch (e.g. one integration added), `materialize_skills()` WILL walk the full skill tree and call `matches_text()` on each file.

### 3. I/O Cost on Steady-State Sandbox Acquire

**Scenario:** User acquires sandbox, skills library is unchanged, integration set unchanged, instructions unchanged.

**Sandbox Acquire Path (lifecycle.py:495-568):**
1. Try to reuse cached sandbox (lines 504-510) — if hit, skip to yield
2. If fresh create (line 524 == True):
   - Call `_seed_user_subtrees(user_id)` (line 530)
     - `ensure_user_workspace(user_id)` (juicefs.py:111-122)
       - Calls `_mkdir()` in thread: stat `.gaia`, mkdir both `.gaia` and user root, return
       - **I/O:** 2-3 mkdir calls (idempotent, stat checks), typically near-zero cost if dirs exist
     - `ensure_user_skills_dir(user_id)` (juicefs.py:125-134)
       - Calls `_mkdir()` in thread: stat + mkdir on skills root
       - **I/O:** 1-2 mkdir calls
   - Call `_create_fresh_sandbox()` (line 531) — creates E2B VM, NOT workspace sync
   - Write canary file (line 540)

**Workspace Sync NOT Called on Sandbox Acquire:**
- `provision_user_workspace()` is NOT called in the sandbox acquire path
- It is only called during:
  - User signup (fire-and-forget)
  - Integration connect/disconnect (fire-and-forget)
  - Startup stale-scan (fire-and-forget, background)

**Conclusion:** On a fresh sandbox create, no workspace/skill sync runs. Only mkdir operations for directory structure.

**On Cached Sandbox Reuse (line 504-510):**
- Zero new I/O for workspace sync
- Only health checks and canary verification

### 4. I/O Cost When Workspace Sync DOES Run

**Scenario:** `materialize_user_integrations()` is called (e.g., during startup stale-scan or integration change).

**Path (lifecycle.py:99-124):**
1. Read `library_hash()` (memory, ~1 dict lookup)
2. Fetch all instructions from DB (async, already happening)
3. Hash instructions (CPU)
4. Call `_materialize_if_stale()` (lifecycle.py:49-81)

**In `_materialize_if_stale()` (sync function, runs in thread):**
1. `read_skills_marker(u_root)` — stat + read `.gaia/skills.v` ✓
2. `read_text_or_none(connected_marker_path)` — stat + read `.gaia/connected.v` ✓
3. `read_text_or_none(instructions_marker_path)` — stat + read `.gaia/instructions.v` ✓
4. **If all 3 markers match expected values → RETURN (no further I/O)**
5. **If any mismatch:**
   - `materialize_skills(u_root, connected)` (skills.py:108-136)
     - `skills_by_subagent()` — in-memory registry lookup
     - For each integration with skills:
       - `_write_skill_dir(skills_dir / skill.slug, skill)` per skill
         - `matches_text(target, skill.body)` — stat + read existing SKILL.md body file
         - If matches → skip write
         - If mismatch → unlink + write
         - For each resource in skill: `matches_text()` check, then write if changed
       - `_write_connected_marker(agent_dir, connected=iid in connected_ids)` — stat + write/unlink `.connected` marker
     - `matches_text(integrations_root / GUIDE_FILENAME, INTEGRATIONS_GUIDE_MD)` — stat + read GUIDE.md
     - If mismatch → write GUIDE.md
   - `materialize_instructions()` (skills.py:139-182)
     - For each integration with instructions:
       - `matches_text(target, content)` — stat + read existing instructions.md
       - If mismatch → write
     - Prune stale instructions (iterate `integrations_root.glob("*/agent")`)
   - `write_skills_marker()` — write `.gaia/skills.v`
   - `write_text()` on `.gaia/connected.v`
   - `write_text()` on `.gaia/instructions.v`

**Worst Case (All Markers Mismatch, Many Skills):**
- 3 marker reads (early gate)
- N skill bodies × (stat + read to check if changed)
- M integration instructions × (stat + read to check if changed)
- 1-5 writes (markers, guides, changed bodies/instructions)
- Glob scan of integrations/ for pruning

**Best Case (All Markers Match):**
- 3 marker reads only
- **0 skill body reads/writes**
- **0 instructions reads/writes**

### 5. Key Files and Functions

| File | Function | Line | Purpose |
|------|----------|------|---------|
| `workspace_sync.py` | `sync_stale_user_workspaces()` | 61 | Startup stale-scan loop, reads markers to gate sync |
| `workspace_sync.py` | `resync_stale_user_workspaces()` | 114 | Startup entry point (async) |
| `sessions/lifecycle.py` | `provision_user_workspace()` | 126 | Top-level user workspace provisioning |
| `sessions/lifecycle.py` | `materialize_user_integrations()` | 99 | Hash-gated skill/instructions materialization |
| `sessions/lifecycle.py` | `_materialize_if_stale()` | 49 | 3-signature gate, calls materializers on mismatch |

| `sessions/skills.py` | `materialize_instructions()` | 139 | Writes instructions.md (calls `matches_text()` per file) |

| `_vfs_common.py` | `read_marker()` | 114 | Reads marker file safely |

| `oauth_service.py` | `store_user_info()` | 42 | New user signup, calls `schedule_user_provision()` at line 136 |

### 6. Summary: Per-Acquire I/O on Steady State

**Fresh Sandbox Create (Unchanged Skills):**
- `_seed_user_subtrees()` — 2-3 mkdir calls to ensure dirs exist
- **No workspace sync runs** (not called from acquire path)
- Total: ~2 stat + 2 mkdir on JuiceFS

**Cached Sandbox Reuse (Unchanged Skills):**
- **Zero workspace sync I/O**
- Only canary/health checks (not shown above)

**Startup Stale-Scan (Unchanged Skills):**
- 3 marker reads (skills.v, connected.v, instructions.v)
- Early exit if all match
- **Total: 3 stat + 3 read on unchanged path**

**Integration Change (Sync Runs):**
- 3 marker reads
- N skill bodies: stat + read each (matches_text)

- Writes only on mismatch
- Glob scan for pruning

toolUseResult.prompt:
I'm optimizing how the GAIA API syncs a user's workspace files (skills, system docs, integration catalogs) onto JuiceFS, to avoid redundant work that generates JuiceFS metadata traffic. I need a precise map of WHEN sync runs and WHAT gating already exists.

Investigate apps/api (FastAPI). Focus:

1. Find `provision_user_workspace` and `sync_user_workspaces` / `sync_stale_user_workspaces` (likely in app/services/workspace_sync.py and app/services/storage/sessions/). Read them.

2. Determine the TRIGGER POINTS — where is workspace/skill provisioning invoked from? Specifically:
   - On every sandbox acquire? (check app/services/sandbox/lifecycle.py acquire_sandbox path and app/services/storage/sessions/lifecycle.py)
   - On every chat turn / agent run?
   - At startup only?
   - On integration connect/disconnect events?
   Trace the actual callers with file:line.

3. What gating/short-circuiting already exists to skip work when nothing changed? Look for:
   - hash markers: SKILLS_HASH_MARKER, read_skills_marker/write_skills_marker, _library_signature, the ".gaia/skills.v" marker
   - matches_text hash comparisons in materialize_skills (app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py)
   - any "signature" or "version" gating
   Explain exactly what each gate compares and what I/O it still does even on the no-op path (e.g. does it still stat/read marker files on JuiceFS on every call?).

4. The key question: on a STEADY-STATE sandbox acquire (skills unchanged), how many JuiceFS filesystem operations (stat/read/scandir/write) does the provision path still perform? Is the gate cheap (one marker read) or does it walk the whole skill tree every time?

Report concrete file:line references, the gating logic, and the residual per-acquire I/O cost on the unchanged path. Do NOT propose fixes — just give me the factual map. Be thorough; read the real files.

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have enough information. Let me compile the comprehensive report:

## GAIA API Workspace Sync Investigation Report

### 1. TRIGGER POINTS — Where Workspace Syncing Is Invoked

#### **Startup (Main App Only)**
- **File:** `app/core/provider_registration.py:257`
- **Trigger:** `resync_stale_user_workspaces()` spawned as background task during `unified_startup(context="main_app")`
- **Path:** `provider_registration.py:257` → spawns `resync_stale_user_workspaces()` → calls `sync_stale_user_workspaces(active_only=True)` (workspace_sync.py:119)

#### **User Registration/Signup**
- **File:** `app/services/oauth/oauth_service.py:136`
- **Trigger:** After successful user creation in `store_user_info()`, fire-and-forget call to `schedule_user_provision(str(result.inserted_id))`
- **Details:** Non-blocking; runs in background. Calls `_provision(user_id)` which calls `provision_user_workspace(user_id)` with no integration IDs (workspace_sync.py:37-40)

#### **Integration Connect/Disconnect**
- **File:** `app/services/integrations/user_integration_status.py:65`
- **Trigger:** When `update_user_integration_status()` sets status to `"connected"`, immediately calls `schedule_user_integrations_sync(user_id)`
- **Path:** `user_integration_status.py:65` → `schedule_user_integrations_sync()` (from integrations_fs.py:27-29) → `sync_user_integrations(user_id)` → `materialize_user_integrations(user_id, connected_ids)`
- **Details:** Fire-and-forget scheduler. Also called on integration disconnect (when status changes from connected).

#### **Sandbox Acquire — Pre-Creation on Host Only (NOT on Cache Hit)**
- **File:** `app/services/sandbox/lifecycle.py:530`
- **Trigger:** When creating a FRESH sandbox (not reusing cached), calls `_seed_user_subtrees(user_id)` which calls:
  - `ensure_user_workspace(user_id)` — creates `{MOUNT}/users/{user_id}` + `.gaia` dir
  - `ensure_user_skills_dir(user_id)` — creates `{MOUNT}/skills/{user_id}` dir
- **Context:** Runs on HOST side (juicefs.py:111-134), not in sandbox. Only for fresh create; cached sandboxes skip this entirely.
- **I/O:** Just `mkdir` operations, no sync of skill contents.

#### **Startup Stale-Scan (Active Users Only)**
- **File:** `app/services/workspace_sync.py:61-103`
- **Logic:** 
  1. On startup, scan users with conversation activity in past N days (settings.SESSION_RETENTION_DAYS, default)
  2. For each user, read `.gaia/skills.v` marker and compare to current `library_hash()`
  3. If mismatch OR `force=True`: call `provision_user_workspace(user_id, connected_ids)`
  4. Handles one deploy-time skill update → reshuffles all active users in background

### 2. GATING/SHORT-CIRCUITING LOGIC — What Avoids Redundant Work

#### **Gate 1: Skills Library Hash (Global)**
- **Marker:** `.gaia/skills.v` under user workspace root
- **Read Location:** `app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py:46-54` (`read_skills_marker()`)
- **Compare:** `workspace_sync.py:85` and `lifecycle.py:63` read marker, compare to `library_hash()` (from `app/agents/workspace/skill_loader.py`)
- **Behavior:**
  - If marker matches current hash → entire `_materialize_if_stale()` returns early at lifecycle.py:74 (no I/O on the "no change" path)
  - If marker missing or mismatch → proceeds to materialize

**I/O Cost (No-Op Path):**
- 1x `read()` call on `.gaia/skills.v` (one stat + read, typically <100 bytes)
- 1x `read()` call on `.gaia/connected.v` marker (connected integration set; typically <1KB)
- 1x `read()` call on `.gaia/instructions.v` marker (instructions hash; typically <64 bytes)
- **Total: 3 small marker reads on steady-state**

#### **Gate 2: Connected Integration Set Signature**
- **Marker:** `.gaia/connected.v` under user workspace root
- **Compare:** `lifecycle.py:66-68` builds `",".join(sorted(connected))` signature, compares at line 71
- **Behavior:** If this differs, proceeds to rewrite SKILL.md catalog (even if library hash matched)

#### **Gate 3: Per-User Instructions Hash**
- **Marker:** `.gaia/instructions.v` under user workspace root
- **Compare:** `lifecycle.py:68` and `72` — compares instructions signature
- **Behavior:** If this differs, proceeds to rewrite instructions.md files

**Combined Gate Logic (lifecycle.py:69-74):**
```python
if (
    current == expected_hash  # Gate 1: library hash
    and previous_connected == connected_signature  # Gate 2: integration set
    and previous_instructions == instructions_sig  # Gate 3: instructions
):
    return  # EARLY EXIT — no I/O below
```
All three must match to early-exit. If ANY differ, materializers run.

#### **Gate 4: Per-File Hash (Inside Materializers)**
- **Location:** `app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py:72-74` (`_write_skill_dir()`) and line 117, 170
- **Function:** `app/services/storage/_vfs_common.py:169-182` (`matches_text()`)
- **Behavior:**
  - Before writing each skill body file (SKILL.md), read target file and compare hash
  - If content unchanged → skip write, count stays at 0
  - **BUT:** This comparison STILL HAPPENS even when Gate 1-3 matched (no early exit above)
  
**Critical Issue:** If Gate 1-3 early-exit fires, `materialize_skills()` and `materialize_instructions()` are never called, so per-file reads are skipped.
However, if Gate 1-3 mismatch (e.g. one integration added), `materialize_skills()` WILL walk the full skill tree and call `matches_text()` on each file.

### 3. I/O Cost on Steady-State Sandbox Acquire

**Scenario:** User acquires sandbox, skills library is unchanged, integration set unchanged, instructions unchanged.

**Sandbox Acquire Path (lifecycle.py:495-568):**
1. Try to reuse cached sandbox (lines 504-510) — if hit, skip to yield
2. If fresh create (line 524 == True):
   - Call `_seed_user_subtrees(user_id)` (line 530)
     - `ensure_user_workspace(user_id)` (juicefs.py:111-122)
       - Calls `_mkdir()` in thread: stat `.gaia`, mkdir both `.gaia` and user root, return
       - **I/O:** 2-3 mkdir calls (idempotent, stat checks), typically near-zero cost if dirs exist
     - `ensure_user_skills_dir(user_id)` (juicefs.py:125-134)
       - Calls `_mkdir()` in thread: stat + mkdir on skills root
       - **I/O:** 1-2 mkdir calls
   - Call `_create_fresh_sandbox()` (line 531) — creates E2B VM, NOT workspace sync
   - Write canary file (line 540)

**Workspace Sync NOT Called on Sandbox Acquire:**
- `provision_user_workspace()` is NOT called in the sandbox acquire path
- It is only called during:
  - User signup (fire-and-forget)
  - Integration connect/disconnect (fire-and-forget)
  - Startup stale-scan (fire-and-forget, background)

**Conclusion:** On a fresh sandbox create, no workspace/skill sync runs. Only mkdir operations for directory structure.

**On Cached Sandbox Reuse (line 504-510):**
- Zero new I/O for workspace sync
- Only health checks and canary verification

### 4. I/O Cost When Workspace Sync DOES Run

**Scenario:** `materialize_user_integrations()` is called (e.g., during startup stale-scan or integration change).

**Path (lifecycle.py:99-124):**
1. Read `library_hash()` (memory, ~1 dict lookup)
2. Fetch all instructions from DB (async, already happening)
3. Hash instructions (CPU)
4. Call `_materialize_if_stale()` (lifecycle.py:49-81)

**In `_materialize_if_stale()` (sync function, runs in thread):**
1. `read_skills_marker(u_root)` — stat + read `.gaia/skills.v` ✓
2. `read_text_or_none(connected_marker_path)` — stat + read `.gaia/connected.v` ✓
3. `read_text_or_none(instructions_marker_path)` — stat + read `.gaia/instructions.v` ✓
4. **If all 3 markers match expected values → RETURN (no further I/O)**
5. **If any mismatch:**
   - `materialize_skills(u_root, connected)` (skills.py:108-136)
     - `skills_by_subagent()` — in-memory registry lookup
     - For each integration with skills:
       - `_write_skill_dir(skills_dir / skill.slug, skill)` per skill
         - `matches_text(target, skill.body)` — stat + read existing SKILL.md body file
         - If matches → skip write
         - If mismatch → unlink + write
         - For each resource in skill: `matches_text()` check, then write if changed
       - `_write_connected_marker(agent_dir, connected=iid in connected_ids)` — stat + write/unlink `.connected` marker
     - `matches_text(integrations_root / GUIDE_FILENAME, INTEGRATIONS_GUIDE_MD)` — stat + read GUIDE.md
     - If mismatch → write GUIDE.md
   - `materialize_instructions()` (skills.py:139-182)
     - For each integration with instructions:
       - `matches_text(target, content)` — stat + read existing instructions.md
       - If mismatch → write
     - Prune stale instructions (iterate `integrations_root.glob("*/agent")`)
   - `write_skills_marker()` — write `.gaia/skills.v`
   - `write_text()` on `.gaia/connected.v`
   - `write_text()` on `.gaia/instructions.v`

**Worst Case (All Markers Mismatch, Many Skills):**
- 3 marker reads (early gate)
- N skill bodies × (stat + read to check if changed)
- M integration instructions × (stat + read to check if changed)
- 1-5 writes (markers, guides, changed bodies/instructions)
- Glob scan of integrations/ for pruning

**Best Case (All Markers Match):**
- 3 marker reads only
- **0 skill body reads/writes**
- **0 instructions reads/writes**

### 5. Key Files and Functions

| File | Function | Line | Purpose |
|------|----------|------|---------|
| `workspace_sync.py` | `sync_stale_user_workspaces()` | 61 | Startup stale-scan loop, reads markers to gate sync |
| `workspace_sync.py` | `resync_stale_user_workspaces()` | 114 | Startup entry point (async) |
| `sessions/lifecycle.py` | `provision_user_workspace()` | 126 | Top-level user workspace provisioning |
| `sessions/lifecycle.py` | `materialize_user_integrations()` | 99 | Hash-gated skill/instructions materialization |
| `sessions/lifecycle.py` | `_materialize_if_stale()` | 49 | 3-signature gate, calls materializers on mismatch |
| `sessions/skills.py` | `read_skills_marker()` | 46 | Reads `.gaia/skills.v` marker |
| `sessions/skills.py` | `materialize_skills()` | 108 | Writes skill bodies (calls `matches_text()` per file) |
| `sessions/skills.py` | `materialize_instructions()` | 139 | Writes instructions.md (calls `matches_text()` per file) |
| `_vfs_common.py` | `matches_text()` | 169 | Stat + read file to check if content differs |
| `_vfs_common.py` | `read_marker()` | 114 | Reads marker file safely |
| `sandbox/lifecycle.py` | `_seed_user_subtrees()` | 411 | Pre-creates workspace dirs on fresh sandbox create |
| `juicefs.py` | `ensure_user_workspace()` | 111 | Creates user workspace dir tree |
| `integrations_fs.py` | `schedule_user_integrations_sync()` | 27 | Fire-and-forget scheduler for integration changes |
| `oauth_service.py` | `store_user_info()` | 42 | New user signup, calls `schedule_user_provision()` at line 136 |

### 6. Summary: Per-Acquire I/O on Steady State

**Fresh Sandbox Create (Unchanged Skills):**
- `_seed_user_subtrees()` — 2-3 mkdir calls to ensure dirs exist
- **No workspace sync runs** (not called from acquire path)
- Total: ~2 stat + 2 mkdir on JuiceFS

**Cached Sandbox Reuse (Unchanged Skills):**
- **Zero workspace sync I/O**
- Only canary/health checks (not shown above)

**Startup Stale-Scan (Unchanged Skills):**
- 3 marker reads (skills.v, connected.v, instructions.v)
- Early exit if all match
- **Total: 3 stat + 3 read on unchanged path**

**Integration Change (Sync Runs):**
- 3 marker reads
- N skill bodies: stat + read each (matches_text)
- M instructions: stat + read each
- Writes only on mismatch
- Glob scan for pruning

The **key optimization** is the 3-signature gate in `_materialize_if_stale()` (lifecycle.py:69-74): if all three match, the function returns immediately without walking the skill tree or reading individual file bodies. This keeps steady-state cost to 3 small marker reads per sync call.

content.text:
Done with the cleanups. Here's where each of your three asks landed.

## 1. "Don't sync skills if they don't change" — already handled

I traced the full sync path. Good news: **skill/system sync is already event-driven and hash-gated — it does *not* run per sandbox-acquire or per chat turn.** It runs only on:
- **startup** (`resync_stale_user_workspaces`, active users only),
- **user signup** (fire-and-forget),
- **integration connect/disconnect** (fire-and-forget).

And every one of those goes through a 3-signature gate (`_materialize_if_stale`, `sessions/lifecycle.py:69`): it reads three tiny marker files (`.gaia/skills.v`, `.gaia/connected.v`, `.gaia/instructions.v`) and **early-exits with zero further I/O** when the library hash + connected-integration set + instructions are unchanged. On a sandbox acquire, the only workspace I/O is idempotent `mkdir`s — no skill walk.

So there's no frequent redundant skill sync to remove — adding more gating would be speculative code for a path that's already cold. The one thing that *was* truly per-acquire is the `/_system` JuiceFS daemon, and the bake already eliminates that.

The *only* marginal tweak available: gate the whole startup active-user scan behind a single global "last-synced library hash" so a redeploy with an unchanged skill library skips the per-user marker reads entirely. It's minor (a few reads per active user, once per boot) and changes re-sync semantics slightly, so I left it out rather than add it on spec. Say the word if you want it.

## 2. Build the template + override locally — I'm blocked on the key

I **cannot** build it: `E2B_API_KEY` is not in `apps/api/.env`, `.env.example`, `mise`, or the shell — and `build_e2b_template.py` hard-exits without it. Building also creates a real, billable cloud template, so I won't fake it.

Once you have the key, this builds it and prints the new template ID:
```bash
cd apps/api && E2B_API_KEY=<your-key> uv run python scripts/build_e2b_template.py
```
Then override locally (current value is `0tnfu1c42a4xd07bsg5p`):
```
# apps/api/.env
E2B_TEMPLATE_ID=<new-id-printed-above>
```
If you export `E2B_API_KEY` into my shell (or add it to `apps/api/.env`), I'll run the build and update `.env` for you.

## 3. PR description — but first, a problem with *which* PR

The open PR on this branch is **#783 "feat(gmail): inbox summary tool…"** — a Gmail feature. The whole `insane-gmail` history is Gmail/files/storage work, and there are **unrelated uncommitted Gmail edits** in the tree right now (`compaction.py`, `factory.py`, `summarization.py`, `gmail_constants.py`) that aren't mine.

My JuiceFS/sandbox changes (`lifecycle.py`, `build_e2b_template.py`, `mount_juicefs.sh`, `.gitignore`) are **unrelated to the Gmail PR** and shouldn't ride in it — mixing them makes #783 unreviewable and couples a Gmail merge to an E2B template rebuild. They belong on their own branch + PR off `develop`.

I won't commit/create a PR unprompted (the tree mixes unrelated changes — I'd risk pulling the Gmail edits in). Here's the ready-to-use description for the dedicated PR:

> **fix(sandbox): cut JuiceFS/Redis metadata load from per-sandbox mounts**
>
> Per-sandbox JuiceFS clients were generating ~constant metadata-engine traffic (dominated by `SMEMBERS`/`ZSCORE`) even while idle.
>
> - **`--no-bgjob` on the primary RW sandbox mount.** Background GC (stale-session scan, trash/slice cleanup) must run on one client per volume; the host-side whole-volume mount is the GC owner. Each sandbox's RW client was independently scanning the whole session set — the main multiplier. (RO mounts already imply `--no-bgjob`.)
> - **Graceful JuiceFS unmount on eviction** so killed sandboxes deregister their metadata sessions instead of leaking and being re-scanned.
> - **Bake the shared `/_system` files into the E2B template** and serve them via a read-only bind mount — removes a per-sandbox read-only JuiceFS daemon for static, release-pinned content. Backward-compatible: falls back to the JuiceFS `/_system` mount on pre-bake templates.
>
> **⚠️ Requires an E2B template rebuild to deploy:** `cd apps/api && uv run python scripts/build_e2b_template.py`, then set the new `E2B_TEMPLATE_ID`. Until rebuilt, sandboxes safely use the JuiceFS fallback.

Want me to **create a clean branch off `develop` with only the 4 sandbox/JuiceFS files** (leaving the Gmail edits out) and open that PR? Or do you actually want these in #783?

31	
32	⏺ Short answer: no, JuiceFS isn't misbehaving — but you're leaving the single 
33	  biggest lever completely untouched, and that's why swapping Postgres → Redis 
34	  didn't help. Here's what the code actually shows.
35	
36	  Why it hammers the metadata engine (and why Redis didn't fix it)
37	
38	  JuiceFS splits a filesystem into two halves: data goes to R2, metadata (the
39	  entire POSIX tree — every inode, dentry, attr) lives in the metadata engine.
40	  Every metadata op — lookup, getattr, readdir, open, create, rename, unlink —
41	  is a transaction against that engine. A coding agent doing git status, npm 
42	  install, recursive ls, builds, etc. generates a firehose of these. That's the
43	  architecture working as designed, not a bug.
44	
45	  That's also the tell: you swapped to Redis and it still got hammered. If the
46	  engine choice were the problem, Redis (the fastest supported tier) would've
47	  absorbed it. It didn't, because the problem isn't engine speed — it's the
48	  volume of metadata operations, and swapping engines doesn't reduce the op
49	  count by one. Postgres is the slowest supported metadata tier so it feels
50	  worst, but the root cause is upstream of the engine.
51	
52	  What you're actually doing wrong
53	
54	  1. Zero metadata caching is configured. I grepped the whole mount path —
55	  apps/api/scripts/mount_juicefs.sh sets --cache-dir/--cache-size/--buffer-size
56	  (those are data caches) but there is not a single --attr-cache, --entry-cache,
57	  --dir-entry-cache, or --open-cache flag anywhere in the repo. So all three
58	  daemons run with JuiceFS defaults: attr/entry/dir-entry caches at ~1 second,
59	  and --open-cache disabled entirely. That means a repeated stat 0.5s apart, or
60	  every open(), round-trips to Postgres. (Verify the exact defaults against your
61	  juicefs version with juicefs mount -h — but they're tiny.)
62	
63	  2. Your isolation model makes aggressive caching SAFE — and you're not 
64	  exploiting it. The usual reason to keep these caches at ~1s is multi-client
65	  coherence. But look at your topology:
66	  - Each /users/$USER_ID subtree is mounted by exactly one sandbox client at a 
67	  time → no cross-client staleness possible within a subtree.
68	  - /skills/$USER_ID and /_system are mounted --read-only and are near-immutable
69	  → they can cache metadata almost indefinitely, yet today every sandbox
70	  re-fetches their attrs every second.
71	
72	  So you can crank --attr-cache, --entry-cache, --dir-entry-cache way up (e.g.
73	  300s) on the user mount, and enable a large --open-cache on the two read-only
74	  mounts, with essentially zero correctness risk. This is the high-leverage fix
75	  and it'll cut steady-state metadata queries dramatically.
76	
77	  3. A second, separate load source: client/connection churn. Every sandbox
78	  acquire launches three juicefs daemons, and your own comments note each pays
79	  "~13s connect + per-table schema introspection." Three new metadata clients —
80	  each opening a Postgres connection pool and re-introspecting the schema — per 
81	  sandbox, on every cold-start/resume. Under sandbox churn that's a storm of
82	  connects + schema reads that's independent of the steady-state op volume. With
83	  Postgres specifically this also pressures max_connections. (Your prod path is
84	  better here — the juicedata/juicefs volume plugin does one mount per node,
85	  not three per sandbox.)
86	
87	  Good news: artifact detection is not a culprit — artifact_watcher.py tails the
88	  FUSE-native .accesslog and debounces, rather than polling the tree with
89	  find/walk. That would've been a metadata firehose; you avoided it.
90	
91	  Recommended order of attack
92	
93	  1. Add metadata cache flags to all three mounts in mount_juicefs.sh (biggest
94	  win, low risk given single-writer-per-subtree). Long caches on the RO
95	  skills/system mounts especially.
96	  2. Stay on whichever engine you prefer — but if you want the headroom,
97	  Redis/TiKV genuinely is the faster tier once caching has cut the volume. Don't
98	  switch expecting it to fix this alone.
99	  3. Reduce client churn — tune the SQL DSN's connection limits, and consider
100	  whether the three-daemon-per-sandbox model can move toward the
101	  host/volume-plugin model the prod compose already uses.
102	
103	  Want me to write up the exact cache-flag changes for mount_juicefs.sh (with
104	  values reasoned per-mount) — or dig into the connection-churn side first?
105	
106	✻ Cogitated for 2m 46s
107	
108	※ recap: You wanted to know why JuiceFS hammers Postgres/Redis. Diagnosis: it's the volume of metadata ops, and you have zero metadata caching configured despite a single-writer-per-subtree setup that makes aggressive caching safe. Next: I write the cache-flag changes for mount_juicefs.sh. (disable recaps in /config)

⏺ Short answer: no, JuiceFS isn't misbehaving — but you're leaving the single 
  biggest lever completely untouched, and that's why swapping Postgres → Redis 
  didn't help. Here's what the code actually shows.

  Why it hammers the metadata engine (and why Redis didn't fix it)

  JuiceFS splits a filesystem into two halves: data goes to R2, metadata (the
  entire POSIX tree — every inode, dentry, attr) lives in the metadata engine.
  Every metadata op — lookup, getattr, readdir, open, create, rename, unlink —
  is a transaction against that engine. A coding agent doing git status, npm 
  install, recursive ls, builds, etc. generates a firehose of these. That's the
  architecture working as designed, not a bug.

  That's also the tell: you swapped to Redis and it still got hammered. If the
  engine choice were the problem, Redis (the fastest supported tier) would've
  absorbed it. It didn't, because the problem isn't engine speed — it's the
  volume of metadata operations, and swapping engines doesn't reduce the op
  count by one. Postgres is the slowest supported metadata tier so it feels
  worst, but the root cause is upstream of the engine.

  What you're actually doing wrong

  1. Zero metadata caching is configured. I grepped the whole mount path —
  apps/api/scripts/mount_juicefs.sh sets --cache-dir/--cache-size/--buffer-size
  (those are data caches) but there is not a single --attr-cache, --entry-cache,
  --dir-entry-cache, or --open-cache flag anywhere in the repo. So all three
  daemons run with JuiceFS defaults: attr/entry/dir-entry caches at ~1 second,
  and --open-cache disabled entirely. That means a repeated stat 0.5s apart, or
  every open(), round-trips to Postgres. (Verify the exact defaults against your
  juicefs version with juicefs mount -h — but they're tiny.)

  2. Your isolation model makes aggressive caching SAFE — and you're not 
  exploiting it. The usual reason to keep these caches at ~1s is multi-client
  coherence. But look at your topology:
  - Each /users/$USER_ID subtree is mounted by exactly one sandbox client at a 
  time → no cross-client staleness possible within a subtree.
  - /skills/$USER_ID and /_system are mounted --read-only and are near-immutable
  → they can cache metadata almost indefinitely, yet today every sandbox
  re-fetches their attrs every second.

  So you can crank --attr-cache, --entry-cache, --dir-entry-cache way up (e.g.
  300s) on the user mount, and enable a large --open-cache on the two read-only
  mounts, with essentially zero correctness risk. This is the high-leverage fix
  and it'll cut steady-state metadata queries dramatically.

  3. A second, separate load source: client/connection churn. Every sandbox
  acquire launches three juicefs daemons, and your own comments note each pays
  "~13s connect + per-table schema introspection." Three new metadata clients —
  each opening a Postgres connection pool and re-introspecting the schema — per 
  sandbox, on every cold-start/resume. Under sandbox churn that's a storm of
  connects + schema reads that's independent of the steady-state op volume. With
  Postgres specifically this also pressures max_connections. (Your prod path is
  better here — the juicedata/juicefs volume plugin does one mount per node,
  not three per sandbox.)

  Good news: artifact detection is not a culprit — artifact_watcher.py tails the
  FUSE-native .accesslog and debounces, rather than polling the tree with
  find/walk. That would've been a metadata firehose; you avoided it.

  Recommended order of attack

  1. Add metadata cache flags to all three mounts in mount_juicefs.sh (biggest
  win, low risk given single-writer-per-subtree). Long caches on the RO
  skills/system mounts especially.
  2. Stay on whichever engine you prefer — but if you want the headroom,
  Redis/TiKV genuinely is the faster tier once caching has cut the volume. Don't
  switch expecting it to fix this alone.
  3. Reduce client churn — tune the SQL DSN's connection limits, and consider

content.thinking:
I see the user has context from another conversation about JuiceFS performance, specifically that metadata caching flags are the main optimization lever. Now they want me to address preventing JuiceFS background sync operations on the skills mount, which appears to be a specific configuration concern that was cut off mid-explanation. The other conversation recommends aggressive metadata caching with flags like `--attr-cache`, `--entry-cache`, and `--dir-entry-cache` set to 300s on the user RW mount, plus `--open-cache` on the RO mounts, reasoning that single-writer-per-subtree topology makes this safe. But I'm realizing this assumption breaks down in our setup — the host whole-volume mount and the sandbox's scoped mount both access the same `/users/<uid>` subtree concurrently, so we actually have two clients touching the same data, which violates the coherence guarantee that aggressive caching relies on.

This means files written host-side (like uploaded artifacts) could remain invisible in the sandbox for up to the cache TTL, and vice versa with the artifact watcher and session writes. So I need to reconsider the caching strategy for the user RW mount — aggressive metadata caching there introduces real staleness risk in the file-upload and artifact flows, and the other conversation's recommendation doesn't account for this cross-client access pattern.

For the read-only mounts though, the picture is different. The system mount is no longer even a JuiceFS mount after my fix, so that's moot. But skills are still a JuiceFS RO mount, and while they're mostly static, they do change when users install or sync skills host-side, creating the same cross-client coherence problem.

The real question is whether the agent even reads skills through the mount or pulls them from memory. If it's reading through the mount, then caching delays how quickly newly-installed skills become visible. Skill installs are rare and not time-critical, so a modest delay might be acceptable, but 300 seconds is excessive.

The honest engineering position is that `--open-cache` on the skills mount is safe and valuable for RO data, but attr/entry/dir-entry caching needs to be moderate—maybe a few seconds to 10-30 seconds—to keep the host-sandbox coherence window tolerable without hammering the system with repeated stat calls.

The real win for a coding agent comes from caching attrs and entries for even a few seconds, since tools like git, npm, and ls re-stat the same files repeatedly in bursts. The marginal benefit of 300 seconds over 30 seconds is minimal for operation count but huge for staleness risk. There's also the subtlety of negative lookups—npm install stats thousands of non-existent paths during node resolution, and while entry caching helps positive lookups, negative caching behavior matters too.

I need to check the actual JuiceFS 1.3.0 flag names and defaults before making a solid recommendation. After the earlier fix reducing daemons from 3 to 2 per sandbox, client churn is already down by a third.

Here's the key insight though: the user RW subtree isn't actually single-client. The host's whole-volume mount at `/mnt/jfs` is a concurrent second client used by file uploads, host-side reads, and the artifact watcher. So aggressive 300-second caching on the user mount would cause host-to-sandbox file propagation to lag by up to that TTL, breaking upload visibility and artifact detection latency. I should recommend moderate cache values instead—enough to eliminate the same-burst re-stat firehose from the agent without sacrificing propagation speed.

The real tension is bidirectional coherence: host writes (uploads) need to be visible in the sandbox quickly, and sandbox writes need to be visible to the host's artifact watcher and file reads. The upload-then-immediately-use flow demands tight coherence, so aggressive user mount caching is genuinely risky. But the agent's own operations within the sandbox (git, npm, build) dominate the re-stat firehose, not external host writes, so there's room to cache without breaking the critical paths. The key insight is that directory entry cache is the sensitive knob for upload visibility — a 30-second dir-entry TTL means newly uploaded files won't appear in the sandbox for up to 30 seconds, whereas attribute cache on existing files matters less. So the responsible approach is to recommend caching with reasoned per-mount values that account for this cross-client coherence constraint, then propose concrete TTLs and offer to implement them. I should also double-check whether the background sync for skills is already handled correctly and evaluate whether there's a cleaner mechanism for cross-client coherence beyond just tuning cache timeouts. I need to look up the exact cache flag names, their defaults, and how they interact with JuiceFS's coherence model—particularly whether open-cache affects cross-client visibility and how metadata caching handles the agent's own writes versus the host's concurrent changes.

content.text: This is the key insight from the other session: **metadata caching** is the highest-leverage lever for the *op volume* (which is complementary to my `--no-bgjob`, which only killed the idle GC scans). # JuiceFS Mount Metadata Caching Flags

## Cache Flags and Defaults

**--attr-cache=1**
"attributes cache timeout in seconds (default: 1)"

**--entry-cache=1**
"file entry cache timeout in seconds (default: 1)"

**--dir-entry-cache=1**
"dir entry cache timeout in seconds (default: 1)"

**--open-cache=0**
"open file cache timeout in seconds (0 means disable this feature) (default: 0)"

**--negative-entry-cache=0**
"negative lookup (return ENOENT) cache timeout in seconds (default: 0, means disable this feature)"

## Cache Consistency Across Mounts

The documentation does not explicitly address whether metadata caching affects visibility of a client's own writes versus other clients' modifications, nor does it provide guidance on cache consistency between multiple mount points. The reference material focuses on technical specifications without discussing multi-mount coherency behavior.

I'm looking at the cache configuration flags for this filesystem tool—there are five different cache types with their default values, and the documentation seems to cut off while explaining them.

Now I'm thinking through how JuiceFS handles cache coherence between clients. When a client makes its own changes, it invalidates its local kernel cache entries so it sees the updates immediately. Other clients only pick up those changes after the TTL expires—this is the standard FUSE behavior. I'm confident about this model.

I'm starting to map out what cache settings make sense for the specific mount topology here, particularly the user RW mount that runs concurrently with the host's whole-volume mount. The key constraint is that when the host uploads a file, it needs to appear in the sandbox within the bounds of the directory entry and lookup cache timeouts.

The negative-entry-cache is a critical optimization for coding agents since tools like npm, Python imports, and git do thousands of stat calls on non-existent paths. Enabling it even for a few seconds would dramatically reduce the metadata engine load. The tradeoff is that if another client creates a file that was previously stat'd as missing, the sandbox might still return ENOENT until the TTL expires—but this is acceptable since the problematic paths are typically module resolution attempts, not upload targets.

For the user RW mount, I'm balancing the firehose reduction against upload visibility. Most of the benefit comes from low single-digit caches since tools re-stat within the same operation. Going higher gives diminishing returns while increasing staleness for uploaded files. I'm settling on modest values: attr-cache around 20 seconds for existing file stats, entry-cache at 10 seconds, and dir-entry-cache at 5 seconds to keep uploads responsive.

For the skills RO mount, since changes only happen on rare skill installs and the sandbox is read-only, I can cache much more aggressively—300 seconds for most flags. The tradeoff is that newly installed skills won't appear in an existing sandbox for up to 5 minutes, which might be acceptable since skills are typically installed then used in a fresh session anyway. I'm leaning toward a shorter window like 60 seconds for entry and dir-entry caches so new skills show up within a minute, while keeping attr and open caches longer.

The host whole-volume mount is the critical one though—it's where the artifact watcher reads sandbox-written files after detecting them via accesslog. If the host's entry and dir-entry caches are too high, the scandir after an accesslog event might return stale data and miss the just-written file. So the host mount needs tight caches close to the defaults to maintain coherence, and I shouldn't aggressively cache it like I would the read-only mounts.

That said, the artifact watcher's repeated scandirs of the sessions tree do generate metadata ops that show up in the Redis count. There's a real tradeoff: caching reduces the 800k op count and helps with their cost concerns, but it risks staling artifact reads. The key insight is that the user mount has concurrent access from both the host (uploads, artifact reads) and the sandbox (self-reads), so aggressive caching there could break the upload-visibility flow. I need to keep the cross-client knobs modest on the user mount while being more aggressive on the read-only mounts where there's no correctness risk.

Let me draft concrete cache values per mount with clear reasoning, flag the coherence constraints, and confirm the skills-staleness tradeoff before implementing. I should also verify the bootstrap.py config—the host mount doesn't currently have metadata cache flags, so I'd need to add them if I'm caching it, but since I'm keeping it tight, that's minimal. One more thing to clarify: the `read` tool goes through the host mount (same bytes readable host-side), while bash commands in the sandbox use the sandbox user mount, so caching the sandbox mount is where the big win is. I should verify the own-writes-seen-immediately claim empirically since I can't run JuiceFS here, but I'm confident in the FUSE model where kernel attribute and entry timeouts handle cache invalidation for local operations. I'll present concrete recommended caching values with reasoning and flag the skills staleness window as the one genuine tradeoff that needs the user's call, then move forward with implementation and the PR once we align on those values.

content.text:
Read it. That session correctly identifies **metadata caching** as the big lever for *op volume* — which is complementary to what I already shipped (`--no-bgjob` only killed the idle GC scans; it doesn't touch the per-operation firehose from `git`/`npm`/`ls`/builds). But it makes one safety claim that's wrong for our topology, and I want to correct it before we pick values.

## First, your skills-bgsync question

Confirmed handled. JuiceFS doesn't "sync skills" as a feature — it only runs background metadata jobs (GC/trash/stale-session/slice cleanup). On the skills subtree those come from the skills mount, which is `--read-only` (implies `--no-bgjob`) **and** I added `--no-bgjob` explicitly. In prod, background jobs run only on the single host whole-volume mount. So no sandbox client bgsyncs the skills subtree. Nothing more to do there.

## The caching premise that's wrong

That session said the user subtree is *"mounted by exactly one sandbox client → no cross-client staleness."* That's not true here: the **host whole-volume mount (`/mnt/jfs`) is a concurrent second client** on every user subtree. It's used by:
- `write_session_file` — chat **file uploads** land host-side, must appear in the sandbox;
- `read_user_file` — the `read` tool reads **host-side**;
- the **artifact watcher** — scandirs/stats the user tree host-side.

So `--attr-cache=300` on the user mount would mean an uploaded file (written host-side) could be invisible in the sandbox for up to 5 minutes, and the `read` tool (host) could miss a just-written sandbox file. That breaks upload→use and artifact freshness. "Crank everything to 300s" is not safe here.

## The nuance that still makes most of it a win

A JuiceFS/FUSE client **sees its own writes immediately** — the cache TTL only delays seeing the *other* client's changes. The firehose we want to kill is the agent's own `git status`/`npm`/build re-statting **its own files** through the **sandbox** mount → that's self-reads, safe to cache regardless of TTL. The TTL only bounds **cross-client propagation** (host upload → sandbox; sandbox write → host read). So we cache hard where it's self-reads or immutable, and keep the cross-client-sensitive knobs modest. (I'm confident on the self-write semantics from the FUSE model, but it's worth a 2-min empirical check on your Redis instance before we commit to big TTLs.)

Also: the biggest single miss in the current config is **`--negative-entry-cache=0`** (disabled). Coding agents stat *thousands* of non-existent paths (node module resolution, `$PATH` lookups, Python imports) — every one round-trips today. Enabling it is likely a bigger op-count cut than the positive caches.

## Proposed values, per mount (reasoned)

**Sandbox user RW mount** (`/workspace`, self-read firehose; cross-client = uploads):
- `--attr-cache=30` — existing-file stat; cross-client attr changes are rare
- `--entry-cache=10`, `--dir-entry-cache=5` — keep low-ish: these gate **upload visibility** (a new file in a cached dir)
- `--negative-entry-cache=5` — kills the ENOENT firehose; modest so an upload after a negative stat isn't hidden long
- `--open-cache=0` — leave; data is RW

**Sandbox skills RO mount** (`/workspace/skills`, changes only on rare skill install):
- `--attr-cache=300 --entry-cache=120 --dir-entry-cache=120 --negative-entry-cache=60 --open-cache=300`
- **One tradeoff for you to call:** a skill installed mid-session won't appear in an *already-running* sandbox for up to the entry TTL (~2 min). Fine if skill installs precede a new turn/session (usually true). If you want installs instantly live in running sandboxes, I'll drop these to ~15s.

**`/_system`** — now a baked bind mount, no JuiceFS client → N/A (the bake already removed this one entirely).

**Host whole-volume mount** (`bootstrap.py`) — **keep tight** (near defaults). It's the coherence-sensitive *reader* (artifact watcher + `read` tool seeing sandbox writes); caching here would stale those. Maybe `--attr-cache=5` at most; leave entry/dir-entry at 1s.

Net: the firehose-heavy path (sandbox bash) gets cached hard and safely, immutable skills get cached very hard, and the two coherence-critical flows (uploads, artifact reads) stay tight.

## Plan

If you're good with the above (and your call on the skills staleness window), I'll:
1. Add these flags to `mount_juicefs.sh` (and the small host-mount bump in `bootstrap.py`),
2. Create the **new branch off `develop`** with the full set — `--no-bgjob`, graceful unmount, `/_system` bake, **and** these cache flags — and open the PR with the description (including the mandatory template rebuild).

Want me to proceed with these values, or adjust the skills window (2 min vs ~15s) first?

# JuiceFS 1.3.0 Metadata Cache Correctness Report — GAIA per-user sandbox topology

## 0. Ground-truth established (sources)

**JuiceFS docs (authoritative, quoted verbatim where load-bearing):**
- Defaults confirmed from the `juicefs mount` command reference: `--attr-cache=1`, `--entry-cache=1`, `--dir-entry-cache=1`, `--negative-entry-cache=0`, `--open-cache=0` (`0 means disable`), `--open-cache-limit=10000`, `--readdir-cache=false`. **Your stated defaults are correct.**
- Cache guide (`docs/en/guide/cache.md`, raw GitHub main): four kernel options default to 1s each except negative=0. **All four `attr/entry/dir-entry/negative-entry` are KERNEL (FUSE) caches**; `open-cache` is a **client-memory** cache.

**Repo (verified by read):**
- No metadata-cache flags exist anywhere today — `grep` of `app/` + `scripts/` for `attr-cache|entry-cache|dir-entry-cache|open-cache|negative-entry` returns **zero hits**. So all three mounts run JuiceFS defaults (attr=1, entry=1, dir-entry=1, neg=0, open=0) right now. The proposal would be a net increase on the sandbox mounts.
- Mount topology in `scripts/mount_juicefs.sh`: user RW `--subdir /users/$USER_ID` → `/mnt/jfs` → bind `/workspace` (lines 252-277, 343); skills RO `--subdir /skills/$USER_ID --read-only` → bind `/workspace/skills` (279-293, 366); `/_system` is a **plain template bind, no JuiceFS client** (378-381).
- Host whole-volume RW mount at `/mnt/jfs` in `bootstrap.py` `_mount()` (lines 317-331): also **no cache flags**, runs bgjob/GC. This is the second concurrent client on every `/users/<uid>`.

---

## 1. Per-flag semantics (verified)

| Flag | Caches | FUSE mapping | Default |
|---|---|---|---|
| `--attr-cache` | File/dir **attributes** (size, mode, mtime, etc.) returned by `getattr`/`stat` | FUSE `attr_timeout` | 1s |
| `--entry-cache` | **File** name→inode `lookup` result (path resolution of a leaf) | FUSE `entry_timeout` | 1s |
| `--dir-entry-cache` | **Directory** name→inode `lookup` result | FUSE `entry_timeout` (for dir entries) | 1s |
| `--negative-entry-cache` | **ENOENT** results (a `lookup` that found nothing) | FUSE negative `entry_timeout` | 0 (disabled) |
| `--open-cache` | Client-memory file attr + **slice/chunk** info, used by `getattr`+`open` | not a kernel timeout — JuiceFS-internal memory cache | 0 (disabled) |

**Critical limitation (quoted):** *"entry cache is gradually built upon file access and may not contain a complete file list under directory, so readdir calls or ls command cannot utilize this cache."* → **`entry`/`dir-entry` cache only accelerates `lookup`/`stat` of a path you already name. `readdir` (the syscall behind `ls`) is governed separately and is NOT served by these caches** (separate `--readdir-cache`, default false). This is the single most important fact for your cross-client analysis and it inverts a naive reading of the proposal.

---

## 2. THE key question: are a client's OWN writes subject to these TTLs? → **NO. Self-writes are safe to cache at any TTL.**

**Confidence: HIGH** — directly documented.

Quoted from `cache.md`: *"On the initiating client, kernel cache is automatically invalidated upon modification. However, when different mount points access and modify the same file, active kernel cache invalidation is only effective on the client initiating the modifications; other clients can only wait for expiration."*

Mechanism: JuiceFS issues active FUSE cache-invalidation notifications (kernel `fuse_lowlevel_notify_inval_*`) **on the same mount that performed the mutation**. So the moment the agent's bash does `write`/`create`/`rename`/`unlink`/`chmod`, the sandbox user mount's own attr/entry/dir-entry cache for the affected inode/dirent is purged locally — the agent's subsequent `git status`/`npm` re-stat firehose **sees its own writes immediately regardless of attr/entry/dir-entry/open TTL**.

**Therefore the agent self-read-of-self-write path is correctness-insensitive to these values.** You can raise attr/entry/dir-entry on the user RW mount purely for load reduction without risking the agent seeing stale views of its own work. The proposed user-mount values (30/10/5) are *generous* relative to this; they're bounded only by the cross-client flow (§3), not by self-consistency.

Two caveats, both verified:
- This invalidation is **per-mount-instance**. The host mount and the sandbox mount are **different clients** — neither benefits from the other's active invalidation. That is exactly the cross-client staleness in §3.
- `open-cache` is the **one exception**: enabling it *"no longer operates under close-to-open consistency."* Even on the same client it can serve a stale chunk/attr view. You correctly keep `open-cache=0` on the user RW mount. (The skills RO `open-cache=300` is analyzed in §3/§4.)

---

## 3. Cross-client flows — which flag bounds staleness, and by how long

Recall: **`ls`/`readdir` is NOT cached** by entry/dir-entry (§1), and **`open()` always queries the metadata service** (close-to-open: *"open calls will always query metadata service, bypassing local cache"*). These two facts dramatically shrink the real staleness windows below what the TTL numbers suggest.

### Flow 1 — host writes an uploaded file → must appear in the sandbox
`write_session_file` (host, `juicefs.py:163`) writes to `/mnt/jfs/users/<uid>/sessions/<conv>/...` via the **host** mount and `fsync`s. The sandbox **user mount** is a different client, so it gets no active invalidation.

- **`ls /workspace/sessions/<conv>/` in the sandbox**: served by `readdir` → **NOT cached** → sees the new file essentially immediately (one metadata round-trip). The proposed `dir-entry-cache=5` does **not** delay this, because dir-entry cache serves `lookup`, not `readdir`. **Answer to your concrete question: `ls` shows a host-uploaded file with ~0 added latency, not 5s.**
- **`open`/`cat /workspace/sessions/<conv>/upload.txt` by exact path in the sandbox**: `open` bypasses cache (close-to-open) → content is current immediately. The bounded item is the **`lookup`** that precedes open: if the sandbox had *previously* `lookup`ed that exact leaf name and cached an entry, `entry-cache` (proposed 10s) bounds it — but only if a prior lookup populated it. For a freshly uploaded path the sandbox has usually never looked up, so no stale entry exists.
- **Negative-entry interaction (your sub-question — real risk):** If the sandbox `stat`s the exact path *before* the host upload (path not yet existing) **and** `negative-entry-cache>0`, the ENOENT is cached for that TTL and a later access keeps returning "not found" until expiry. With your proposed **user-mount `negative-entry-cache=5`, a pre-stat'd-then-uploaded file stays invisible for up to 5s.** With the JuiceFS default (0) this can't happen. This is the one place the proposal *adds* a staleness window that doesn't exist today. **Verdict: keep user-mount negative-entry at 0, or ≤1s.** Upload-then-immediately-reference is a plausible real flow (UI uploads a file, agent told to read it).

**Flow-1 bounding flag: `negative-entry-cache` (worst case = its TTL). attr/entry/dir-entry are effectively non-binding for the appear-in-listing/open case.**

### Flow 2 — sandbox (agent bash) writes a file → host `read` tool + artifact watcher must see it
Host-side reads go through the **host** mount: `read_user_file` (`juicefs.py:202`, scandir+open under `/mnt/jfs/users/<uid>`), and the watcher's `list_artifacts`/`stat_artifact` (`sessions/artifacts.py:104,124`) which `scandir`+`stat` host-side. The **host mount's** caches bound these, NOT the sandbox mount's.

- **Watcher rescan after an accesslog event** (`artifact_watcher.py:266-305`): on a mutating accesslog line it debounces 0.7s then `list_artifacts` (a `readdir` walk) on the host mount. `readdir` is **not** served by entry/dir-entry cache → the walk sees newly created files immediately. **BUT `stat` on each found file returns `(size, mtime)` used for the change-diff (`artifacts.py:144-148`), and `stat` IS served by `attr-cache`.** With the host mount kept tight (attr ≤5, dir-entry=1 as proposed) the watcher could see a file but read a **stale size/mtime** for up to `attr-cache` seconds, suppressing the upsert (because `prev == current`) until the next rescan after attr expiry. **This is the host-mount attr-cache value directly setting the artifact-streaming staleness floor.** Today (attr=1 default) it's ~1s; your proposal (≤5) makes it up to 5s. Recommend **host attr-cache=1** (keep default) for the snappy ~1-2s artifact latency the module's docstring promises.
- **`read` tool** (`read_user_file`): does `scandir`-free direct `open`+read by path. `open` bypasses cache (current content), but the **preceding `lookup`** of the leaf is bounded by host `entry-cache`, and if the host previously got an ENOENT for that path and host `negative-entry-cache>0`, it'd be stale. Host negative is at default 0 → safe. With host entry-cache=1 (default/your "keep tight") the lookup window is ≤1s. Content itself is correct via open-bypass.

**Flow-2 bounding flag: host-mount `attr-cache` (watcher size/mtime diff) and host `entry-cache` (read-tool lookup). Both should stay at the default 1, not be raised. The sandbox mount's caches are irrelevant to Flow 2.**

---

## 4. Correctness landmines (each verified against code)

1. **Canary staleness check (`lifecycle.py:315-348`) — NOT affected, and NOT a real cross-client coherence test.** `_write_canary`/`_read_canary` both run **inside the same sandbox via the E2B SDK** (`sbx.files.write`/`sbx.files.read` on `/workspace/.gaia/canary.txt`). Same client writes and reads → §2 self-write invalidation applies → never falsely stale due to any TTL. The check's purpose (per docstring at `lifecycle.py:13`, E2B GH#884) is detecting an E2B **filesystem rollback/snapshot divergence**, not JuiceFS cache coherence. **Safe at any proposed value.** (Note: `files.read` from envd reads through the FUSE mount, but it's the writing client, so coherent.)

2. **Pause/resume.** On resume, `_ensure_mounted` (`lifecycle.py:307-312`) re-probes `/workspace`; a wedged/absent mount triggers a full re-`mount.sh`, which starts **fresh juicefs daemons with empty caches**. So resume cannot serve pre-pause stale metadata — caches don't survive the remount. No landmine. (If the mount happens to survive resume intact, the TTLs simply resume counting; still bounded by the same §3 analysis.)

3. **Watcher accesslog→scandir sequence — REAL, partial landmine (see Flow 2).** The accesslog tail (`artifact_watcher.py:253-259`) fires on the **sandbox** mount's FUSE ops, but the rescan reads the **host** mount. A just-`write`+`close`d file's *existence* is caught (readdir uncached), but its **size/mtime can be stale for up to host `attr-cache` seconds**, which can drop the change from the diff until the next trigger. Mitigation: keep **host attr-cache=1**. Do **not** raise host attr-cache to 5; it directly lengthens artifact-stream lag and can miss rapid same-path rewrites within the window. This is the strongest reason to reject the "host attr-cache ≤5" latitude — pin it at 1.

4. **Symlinks into `/workspace/.system`.** `.system` is a **plain bind of a template-baked dir** (`mount_juicefs.sh:378-381`) — no JuiceFS client, no metadata-engine caches at all. Per-user symlinks point into it. Resolving a symlink target under `.system` is local-FS, cache-irrelevant. The symlink *entries themselves* live in the user `/users/<uid>` tree and are created host-side; their visibility in the sandbox follows Flow 1 (readdir uncached → fine; negative-cache pre-stat caveat applies identically). No new landmine beyond the negative-entry one.

5. **`open-cache` on the RO skills mount after a skill reinstall — REAL landmine.** Skills are written **host-side** (`write_skill_file`, `juicefs.py:141`) and read RO in the sandbox. With proposed **skills `open-cache=300`**: per docs, *"with --open-cache enabled, JuiceFS no longer operates under close-to-open consistency."* After a host-side reinstall that **overwrites an existing skill file at the same path**, the sandbox RO mount can serve the **old file's cached slice/attr for up to 300s** even on a fresh `open` — because open-cache short-circuits the metadata query that close-to-open relies on. New files appear fine (readdir uncached; no prior open-cache entry); **in-place overwrites of an already-opened skill file are the danger.** Severity depends on whether reinstall happens within a live sandbox's lifetime and reuses paths. **Verdict: skills `open-cache=300` is RISKY for the reinstall-overwrite case.** If skills are immutable-per-version (new dir per install) it's safe; if they overwrite in place, drop open-cache to a small value (e.g. 10-30s) or 0. **Flag: I could not verify from the provided files whether skill reinstall overwrites in place or writes a new versioned path** — this determines SAFE vs RISKY. Check `app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py` and the skill installer.

6. **Metadata engine (Redis vs SQL) does not change any of this.** These five flags are **client-side FUSE/memory caches**, set per-mount, identical semantics regardless of the metadata engine. Migrating Postgres/Cockroach→Redis changes metadata *latency* (and thus how much these caches *help*), not their *correctness*. No engine-specific coherence concern. Confidence: HIGH.

---

## 5. Final per-flag, per-mount verdict

### User RW mount (sandbox `/workspace`, `/users/<uid>`)
| Flag | Proposed | Verdict | Reason / recommended |
|---|---|---|---|
| attr-cache | 30 | **SAFE** | Self-writes auto-invalidate (§2); doesn't affect host→sandbox visibility (Flow 1 uses readdir/open). 30 fine; pure load win. |
| entry-cache | 10 | **SAFE** | Only bounds `lookup` of a leaf the sandbox already looked up; self-writes invalidate; new host files use readdir. 10 fine. |
| dir-entry-cache | 5 | **SAFE** | Does **not** delay `ls` of host-uploaded files (readdir uncached). Self-writes invalidate. 5 fine. |
| **negative-entry-cache** | **5** | **NEEDS-LOWER-VALUE → 0 (or ≤1)** | Only flag that *adds* a real staleness window: pre-stat'd-then-host-uploaded path stays ENOENT up to 5s (Flow 1). Default 0 has no such hole. |
| open-cache | 0 | **SAFE (keep 0)** | Required to preserve close-to-open for cross-client content coherence. |

### Skills RO mount (sandbox `/workspace/skills`, `/skills/<uid>`)
| Flag | Proposed | Verdict | Reason / recommended |
|---|---|---|---|
| attr-cache | 300 | **SAFE** | RO; only freshness vs host reinstall, and a 300s attr lag on size/mode is acceptable for skills. |
| entry-cache | 120 | **SAFE** | Lookup-only; new skills appear via readdir regardless. |
| dir-entry-cache | 120 | **SAFE** | Same; doesn't gate `ls` of newly installed skills. |
| negative-entry-cache | 60 | **RISKY** | If the sandbox `stat`s a skill path before it's installed host-side, it stays ENOENT for 60s. For a *just-installed* skill the agent is told to use, 60s of "not found" is a plausible bad UX. Recommend ≤10s. |
| **open-cache** | **300** | **RISKY → verify install model** | Breaks close-to-open on the RO mount. Safe **only if** reinstall writes a new versioned path (never overwrites an open file). If installs overwrite in place, stale skill body served ≤300s. Recommend 0-30 until the install path is confirmed. |

### Host whole-volume mount (`/mnt/jfs`, RW, GC owner)
| Flag | Proposed | Verdict | Reason / recommended |
|---|---|---|---|
| **attr-cache** | ≤5 | **NEEDS-LOWER-VALUE → 1** | Directly sets the artifact-watcher size/mtime-diff staleness floor (Flow 2, landmine 3) and the `read` tool's freshness. Keep at default 1; do not use the "≤5" headroom. |
| entry-cache | 1 | **SAFE** | Keep 1 (read-tool lookup window). |
| dir-entry-cache | 1 | **SAFE** | Keep 1. |
| negative-entry-cache | (unset=0) | **SAFE** | Keep 0 — host pre-stat of a sandbox-about-to-create file must not cache ENOENT. |
| open-cache | (unset=0) | **SAFE** | Keep 0 — host must see sandbox writes' content on open. |

---

## 6. Items I could NOT verify (flagged)
- **Skill reinstall write model** (overwrite-in-place vs new versioned path) — decides whether skills `open-cache=300` and host-side overwrite are SAFE or RISKY (§4.5). Need to read `app/services/storage/sessions/skills.py` + the skill installer. **This is the highest-value open question.**
- I relied on JuiceFS docs (`docs/en/guide/cache.md` @ main, command reference) and the documented FUSE-notify invalidation mechanism for the self-write claim; I did **not** run a live two-client write/stat experiment in your actual R2+Redis setup. The self-write-invalidation behavior is documented and standard, but per your "running > reading" bar, a 2-client repro (host write + sandbox `inotify`-free stat loop, and reverse) would convert HIGH→verified. The negative-entry and open-cache staleness windows in particular are worth a direct repro before rollout.

**Key file:line anchors:** mount flags `scripts/mount_juicefs.sh:252-307`; host mount `app/services/storage/bootstrap.py:292-331`; host reads `app/services/storage/juicefs.py:163,202`; watcher diff `app/services/sandbox/artifact_watcher.py:266-305` + `app/services/storage/sessions/artifacts.py:104-152`; canary `app/services/sandbox/lifecycle.py:58,315-348`; `.system` bind `scripts/mount_juicefs.sh:378-381`.

| `--dir-entry-cache` | **Directory** name→inode `lookup` result | FUSE `entry_timeout` (for dir entries) | 1s |
| `--negative-entry-cache` | **ENOENT** results (a `lookup` that found nothing) | FUSE negative `entry_timeout` | 0 (disabled) |
| `--open-cache` | Client-memory file attr + **slice/chunk** info, used by `getattr`+`open` | not a kernel timeout — JuiceFS-internal memory cache | 0 (disabled) |

- This invalidation is **per-mount-instance**. The host mount and the sandbox mount are **different clients** — neither benefits from the other's active invalidation. That is exactly the cross-client staleness in §3.
- `open-cache` is the **one exception**: enabling it *"no longer operates under close-to-open consistency."* Even on the same client it can serve a stale chunk/attr view. You correctly keep `open-cache=0` on the user RW mount. (The skills RO `open-cache=300` is analyzed in §3/§4.)

- **`ls /workspace/sessions/<conv>/` in the sandbox**: served by `readdir` → **NOT cached** → sees the new file essentially immediately (one metadata round-trip).
